an2.10:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two rains retreat observances.
an2.10:1.4: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two rains retreat observances."
an2.10:1.6: The Chapter on Punishments is the first.
an2.10:2.4: Bright, conduct, rains retreat observance — the chapter.
an2.1:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika's park.
an2.1:2.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two kinds of offence.
an2.1:2.3: An offence visible in this present life, and an offence pertaining to the next life.
an2.1:2.4: And what, *bhikkhus*, is an offence visible in this present life?
an2.1:2.5: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone sees a thief, an evildoer, being seized by kings and subjected to various punishments:
an2.1:2.6: being flogged with whips, beaten with canes, struck with split sticks; having a hand cut off, a foot cut off, a hand and foot cut off, an ear cut off, a nose cut off, both ear and nose cut off; subjected to the 'gruel-pot' torture, the 'shell-tonsure', the 'Rāhu's mouth', the 'fire-garland', the 'blazing-hand', the 'grass-tuft', the 'bark-garment', the 'antelope', the 'hook-and-flesh', the 'coin-slice', the 'brine-splash', the 'bar-and-pivot', the 'straw-mattress'; being doused with boiling oil, fed to dogs, impaled alive on stakes, and beheaded with a sword.
an2.1:3.2: 'On account of such evil deeds as these, kings seize thieves and evildoers and subject them to various punishments —
an2.1:3.3: flogging with whips, beating with canes, striking with split sticks; cutting off a hand, cutting off a foot, cutting off a hand and foot, cutting off an ear, cutting off a nose, cutting off both ear and nose; the gruel-pot torture, the shell-tonsure, the Rāhu's mouth, the fire-garland, the blazing-hand, the grass-tuft, the bark-garment, the antelope, the hook-and-flesh, the coin-slice, the brine-splash, the bar-and-pivot, the straw-mattress; dousing with boiling oil, feeding to dogs, impaling alive on stakes, beheading with a sword.
an2.1:3.4: But if I were to do such evil deeds, the kings would seize me too and subject me to all these various punishments —
an2.1:3.5: flogging with whips … beheading with a sword.'
an2.1:3.6: Afraid of the offence visible in this present life, he refrains from going about plundering others' property.
an2.1:3.7: This, *bhikkhus*, is called an offence visible in this present life.
an2.1:4.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is an offence pertaining to the next life?
an2.1:4.10: 'We will be afraid of offences visible in this present life; we will be afraid of offences pertaining to the next life; we will be fearful of offences, seeing the danger in offences.'
an2.1:4.11: This is how you should train yourselves, *bhikkhus*.
an2.1:4.12: For those, *bhikkhus*, who are fearful of offences and who see the danger in offences — it is to be expected that they will be freed from all offences."
an2.1:4.3: 'Misconduct in body has a bad, painful result in the next life; misconduct in speech has a bad, painful result in the next life; misconduct in mind has a bad, painful result in the next life.
an2.1:4.4: But if I were to engage in misconduct in body, misconduct in speech, misconduct in mind —
an2.1:4.5: what is to prevent me, after the breaking up of the body, at death, from being reborn in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell?'
an2.1:4.6: Afraid of the offence pertaining to the next life, he abandons misconduct in body and develops good conduct in body, abandons misconduct in speech and develops good conduct in speech, abandons misconduct in mind and develops good conduct in mind, and keeps himself pure.
an2.1:4.7: This, *bhikkhus*, is called an offence pertaining to the next life.
an2.1:4.8: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two kinds of offence.
an2.1:4.9: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, you should train yourselves thus:
an2.2:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two kinds of striving that are hard to accomplish in the world.
an2.2:1.3: The striving of householders who dwell at home to provide robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and requisites for the sick; and the striving of those who have gone forth from home into homelessness for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.
an2.2:1.4: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two kinds of striving that are hard to accomplish in the world.
an2.2:2.1: Of these two kinds of striving, *bhikkhus*, the foremost is the striving for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.
an2.2:2.2: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, you should train yourselves thus:
an2.2:2.3: 'We will strive with unwavering effort for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.'
an2.2:2.4: This is how you should train yourselves, *bhikkhus*."
an2.3:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two *dhammas* that are tormenting.
an2.3:1.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone has engaged in misconduct in body and has not engaged in good conduct in body;
an2.3:1.4: has engaged in misconduct in speech and has not engaged in good conduct in speech;
an2.3:1.5: has engaged in misconduct in mind and has not engaged in good conduct in mind.
an2.3:1.6: He is tormented thinking 'I have engaged in misconduct in body,' and tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in good conduct in body';
an2.3:1.7: he is tormented thinking 'I have engaged in misconduct in speech,' and tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in good conduct in speech';
an2.3:1.8: he is tormented thinking 'I have engaged in misconduct in mind,' and tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in good conduct in mind.'
an2.3:1.9: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two *dhammas* that are tormenting."
an2.4:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two *dhammas* that are non-tormenting.
an2.4:1.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone has engaged in good conduct in body and has not engaged in misconduct in body;
an2.4:1.4: has engaged in good conduct in speech and has not engaged in misconduct in speech;
an2.4:1.5: has engaged in good conduct in mind and has not engaged in misconduct in mind.
an2.4:1.6: He is not tormented thinking 'I have engaged in good conduct in body,' and not tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in misconduct in body';
an2.4:1.7: he is not tormented thinking 'I have engaged in good conduct in speech,' and not tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in misconduct in speech';
an2.4:1.8: he is not tormented thinking 'I have engaged in good conduct in mind,' and not tormented thinking 'I have not engaged in misconduct in mind.'
an2.4:1.9: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two *dhammas* that are non-tormenting."
an2.5:1.1: "There are two *dhammas*, *bhikkhus*, that I have discerned —
an2.5:1.11: Let only skin and sinew and bone remain; let the flesh and blood dry up in the body — whatever can be attained by human strength, human energy, human endeavour, that we will not stop until we have attained it.'
an2.5:1.12: This is how you should train yourselves, *bhikkhus*."
an2.5:1.2: discontent with wholesome *dhammas*, and unrelenting effort.
an2.5:1.4: 'Let only skin and sinew and bone remain; let the flesh and blood dry up in the body — whatever can be attained by human strength, human energy, human endeavour, that I will not stop until I have attained it.'
an2.5:1.5: And for me, *bhikkhus*, through diligence was awakening attained; through diligence was the unsurpassed security from bondage attained.
an2.5:1.6: If you too, *bhikkhus*, were to strive unrelentingly:
an2.5:1.7: 'Let only skin and sinew and bone remain; let the flesh and blood dry up in the body — whatever can be attained by human strength, human energy, human endeavour, that I will not stop until I have attained it' — then you too, *bhikkhus*, before long —
an2.5:1.8: for the sake of which sons of good family rightly go forth from home into homelessness, that unsurpassed culmination of the holy life — you will, having realized it for yourselves with direct knowledge, here and now enter upon and dwell in it.
an2.5:1.9: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, you should train yourselves thus:
an2.6:1.3: Seeing gratification in things that fetter, and seeing disenchantment in things that fetter.
an2.6:1.4: Dwelling, *bhikkhus*, seeing gratification in things that fetter, one does not abandon lust, does not abandon hatred, does not abandon delusion.
an2.6:1.5: Not having abandoned lust, not having abandoned hatred, not having abandoned delusion, one is not freed from birth, from old age, from death, from sorrows, lamentations, pains, griefs, and distresses.
an2.6:1.6: 'One is not freed from *dukkha*,' I say.
an2.6:2.1: Dwelling, *bhikkhus*, seeing disenchantment in things that fetter, one abandons lust, abandons hatred, abandons delusion.
an2.6:2.2: Having abandoned lust, having abandoned hatred, having abandoned delusion, one is freed from birth, from old age, from death, from sorrows, lamentations, pains, griefs, and distresses.
an2.7:1.3: Lack of conscience and lack of moral concern.
an2.9:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, these two bright *dhammas* that protect the world.
an2.9:1.4: If, *bhikkhus*, these two bright *dhammas* did not protect the world, there would be no recognition of 'mother', 'mother's sister', 'maternal uncle's wife', 'teacher's wife', or 'wives of those deserving respect.'
an2.9:1.5: The world would fall into promiscuity, as among goats and sheep, chickens and pigs, dogs and jackals.
an2.9:1.6: But because, *bhikkhus*, these two bright *dhammas* do protect the world, there is recognition of 'mother', 'mother's sister', 'maternal uncle's wife', 'teacher's wife', and 'wives of those deserving respect.'"
